Essentially, this shift means tailoring platforms to each company's specific needs rather than using a one-size-fits-all SaaS model. So this change isn't surprising. But the market—or rather, investors and speculators—as usual, threw the baby out with the bathwater. Consequently, we've also concentrated our positions in the stocks where we have the highest conviction, as we always do. As I mentioned, most of the volatility is algorithm-driven, and algorithms don't conduct the kind of research we do. That's precisely why we focus on investing in the highest-conviction opportunities—the market has presented us with such chances. That's our perspective on volatility. You may recall that during the tariff turmoil last April, I also stated here: "Look, we believe this market condition is temporary." The market plunged significantly then, and many were terrified—even investors who are usually very calm and accustomed to volatility wavered last year. But anyone who sold during that dramatic moment regretted it for the rest of the year. Since then, the market has rallied strongly. This market is climbing a "wall of worry." Markets that climb walls of worry often produce the strongest bull runs. While volatility is certainly unsettling, this environment is far healthier than what we experienced during the tech and telecom bubble. Back then, Jeff Bezos could come out and say, "We're losing more money because we're investing aggressively. The opportunities in the internet are even greater than we imagined." Then the market would rally, and Amazon's stock would jump 10% to 
15%. That wouldn't happen today. Instead, the "Big Six Tech" (Mag Six) are essentially saying, "We're going to spend more." But investors have grown accustomed to these companies consistently growing their free cash flow over the past five years or so. That dynamic is changing. We're seeing some more traditional investors, focused primarily on free cash flow and margins, getting nervous and starting to reduce their positions. We don't necessarily think that's a good idea. We think Google, Meta, Microsoft, and Amazon should spend aggressively because this is the biggest opportunity we've ever seen. The question is: As we evolve toward agentic AI and chatbots, will this cannibalize time spent on traditional social media? From a shopping perspective, will our smart agents handle everything online, meaning Amazon's cut won't be as significant as it has been recently? We'll wait and see, and we'll certainly monitor market share shifts closely—just as we accurately predicted the SaaS landscape's transformation. Fiscal Policy: Deficits and GDP Outlook Alright, let's start with the charts. I'd characterize the current environment as macroeconomic conditions akin to trade turmoil periods, presenting massive opportunities—as I mentioned earlier, we'll definitely leverage this volatility. Our long-term performance is built precisely on such decisions. Let's examine the charts. The budget deficit as a percentage of GDP briefly dipped below 5%, but this proved fleeting until first-quarter GDP data and forecasts were revised downward. We're now approaching the "4% range." Treasury Secretary Bessent targets 3%. In fact, we are increasingly confident that by the end of this presidential term—late 2028 or early 2029—we will achieve a fiscal surplus. This stems from productivity growth far exceeding expectations. Some examples are downright staggering, like Palantir. Its U.S. commercial revenue surged 142%, while I believe Palantir actually reduced its sales force slightly. This productivity surge is astonishing—at least from a sales perspective, exceeding 100%, even surpassing 140%. I believe this is the mindset we all need to cultivate, a "muscle" we must exercise when discussing how the world and companies operate. Elon Musk sets wildly ambitious targets, and I think that's the right mindset. Companies lacking this mindset will lose opportunities to more agile competitors. We've proposed that by the end of this decade, global real GDP growth could reach 7% to 8%. Many scoffed at this. But given what we're witnessing, this forecast might even be too conservative. It could mean a significant reduction in the deficit as a percentage of GDP. Barring some foolish policies, we'll shift into surplus. The COVID pandemic was a massive panic that deeply unsettled bond markets and investors. Now we're correcting course, hoping to learn lessons and ultimately achieve surpluses like in the 90s—when the last small tech revolution (the internet) occurred. Trade Deficits and the Dollar's Path Next is another deficit. In the 70s and 80s 
, bond markets were highly vigilant about the "twin deficits": the federal budget deficit and the trade deficit. You can see the trade deficit during and after the pandemic: imports surged, plunging us into a massive deficit. As the chart shows, this is also changing. Many in the foreign exchange market use the trade deficit to gauge the dollar's direction. If we import far more than we export, concerns arise that the dollar will weaken. This creates a pivot point in the market. You can see we've corrected this. Of course, imports plummeted earlier due to tariffs and anticipated tariff policies (as businesses rushed to import ahead of time). If our judgment was correct, we're now seeing the opposite effect. The limited extent of the deficit a few months ago surprised me. If we are correct—that the U.S. will exceed expectations in real GDP growth and that U.S. capital returns will rise due to tax cuts, deregulation, and tariffs—then we may see more import growth. Not because of tariffs, of course, but due to the first two factors. We will continue to run deficits. However, we have never truly worried about the trade deficit. Because when you consider the full picture, the flip side of the trade balance (goods and services) is a capital surplus. Given the U.S.'s generally favorable environment for business and capital, we attract funds from the rest of the world. I know people worry about twin deficits, so I specifically address this. Regarding the trade deficit, I've already explained why we aren't concerned. Regarding the federal deficit, we've seen a significant improvement in the deficit as a percentage of GDP. I want to describe the dollar's trajectory. I know this chart has been shown before, and it represents another external concern. You'll hear a lot about the "end of American exceptionalism," which can mean different things to different people. But from an economic standpoint, I would say it hasn't ended. In fact, due to this technology revolution led by the U.S. and China, we may see an explosion of U.S. economic activity. So I genuinely believe the dollar will turn and move in the other direction. Of course, the dollar's decline is largely political. I think countries are diversifying their investments away from the dollar toward gold and other currencies, but we believe this will change. Given the gold price trend, it has reached extremes. But look at what's happening with the dollar. If you look at this chart and zoom out, this isn't a dollar collapse. If you're a technical analyst, you'd say, "Hey, the dollar has stabilized at the expected support level," meaning the previous peak (as shown by the black line). If the dollar rises, one point I'm watching is: a rising dollar acts as a powerful anti-inflationary force. We genuinely believe the dollar will strengthen, leading to lower-than-expected inflation. Inflation, Money Supply, and Fed Policy Here we see CPI (green line, year-over-year percentage change) versus M2. We're still recovering from negative M2 growth, but I believe we've stabilized within the 5% range. As I mentioned earlier, we also see money velocity beginning to level off or decline. A decline or stabilization in velocity would partially unwind inflation expectations tied to money growth. If you look at the green line, it appears stuck in the 2% to 3% range, where it has been for the past several years. We expect it to decline significantly this year. I've been saying this for a long time: I didn't anticipate supply shocks would make inflation so sticky, nor did I expect them to persist for three years—that was unforeseen. But considering the turbulent context at the time, maintaining it within the 2% to 3% range was actually quite good. This may suggest or validate our view that we'll see inflation decline. To help understand monetary policy, here's the short end of the yield curve: the 2-year Treasury yield versus the 3-month Treasury yield. You can see it remains in negative territory, indicating that marginally, the Fed isn't accommodative—it's even a bit too tight. Based on the 10-year versus 2-year Treasury yield metric, it's in positive territory, but you can see it's sloping downward. If the Fed were to aggressively ease policy, you'd expect this line to continue rising. If we start seeing negative inflation data (which I believe we will), the Fed might aggressively ease. We may have to wait until Kevin Warsh takes over before we see negative inflation in the year-over-year data. We know Kevin Warsh is a very disciplined economist from a monetary perspective. Hearing him say, "This AI revolution could accelerate growth in many sectors of the economy, and as monetary authorities, we should accommodate this real growth as long as it doesn't trigger inflation," is very reassuring. If we experience negative inflation (which I believe we will) while real GDP 
rapidly rising, the current Fed administration might tighten policy—that would be a grave mistake. This Fed believes "growth causes inflation." Kevin Warsh is correct: growth does not cause inflation. In fact, it accelerates productivity growth, thereby suppressing inflation. This is the 
story behind the miraculous stock markets of the 1980s and 1990s: real growth accelerated, inflation declined, and productivity was the primary driver. Productivity and wage productivity measure output per hour worked, while unit labor costs represent wages adjusted for productivity. If you examine this chart, our current unit labor cost growth rate is approximately 
1.2%. This is a chart many Keynesian economists have been watching closely, expecting unit labor cost growth to accelerate or remain within the 5% to 7% range. They didn't get productivity right—it was stronger than anticipated; nor did they get wages right—the growth rate was lower than expected. They learned their lesson from the 1960s and 1970s 
. Back then, unit labor costs (as shown in the chart) soared into double digits as workers demanded higher wage increases to offset skyrocketing food and energy prices. Given the supply shocks during the COVID-19 pandemic, they were prepared for the same outcome this time. But as you can see, that hasn't happened. Any Keynesian economist must look at this and say, "This time is different." Despite solid real GDP growth and low unemployment in the aggregate data, we haven't seen unit labor costs rebound. One reason is that workers aren't making the same tough demands as in the 1970s. Another is falling oil prices, which marginally help budgets. A third reason, I believe, is that unionized workers now make up less than 10% of total employment 
10%, whereas in the late 70s and early 80s it was closer to a quarter. Back then, union leaders were the driving force behind substantial increases in wages per hour worked. To be clear: if our prediction of an impending economic boom proves correct, and if opportunities emerge as we anticipate with real growth accelerating, we don't believe unions will be necessary. If companies leverage these new technologies—AI, robotics, and so forth—wage growth will accelerate for a sound reason: it will reflect productivity gains for all workers. Price Trends: Real Estate, Oil, and Inflation Data Let's discuss the next reason we believe inflation won't exceed expectations. Consider real estate prices: the green line showing existing home price inflation has fallen below 1%. 
; new home price inflation (i.e., price inflation tied to new home sales) remains negative; and rents are starting to decline. These data points take a long time to feed into the government's CPI index. Therefore, we believe these price pressures will exert downward pressure on CPI in the coming years. We are quite confident about this. Now consider oil prices, which show double-digit year-over-year declines. This typically occurs during economic recessions. But we now observe this as a global phenomenon. Of course, Saudi Arabia in the Middle East is a swing factor, consistently increasing supply. I believe this may relate to political dynamics arising from negotiations between the Trump administration and Saudi Arabia on various matters, including defense and other programs. Here we separate core CPI (purple) from core PPI (green). If you look closely, you'll see PPI inflation has already outpaced CPI inflation. So those holding consumer goods company stocks in the market may hear more about margin pressures—keep an eye on that. This is the most important slide: Truflation (Real Inflation). It shows inflation is collapsing after years of hovering between 2% and 3%. The current reading is approximately 0.7% year-over-year. This is a real-time indicator tracking 10,000 goods and services. You can see it captured inflation peaks better than CPI—it once approached 12% while CPI was at 9%. One thing we see in the real-time data is that food price inflation is declining. We're seeing deflation in eggs and some food items that caused pain during the pandemic, which is positive. But if you look at today's food prices relative to pre-pandemic levels, they're still about 32% higher. So I believe we'll see food price inflation fall further, which is another reason inflation could turn negative. Real Economy and Consumer Sentiment Regarding real activity, this is the Manufacturing ISM 
(Purchasing Managers' Index). You can see a solid rebound here. We've described the past three years as a "rolling recession." If you look at housing and all U.S. manufacturing, they've been below 50. This essentially indicates manufacturing has been in pain, even if we weren't technically in a recession like when the index is negative—it was a rolling recession. Now, this appears to be the first sign of change. The biggest shift in this PMI is orders. The new orders index jumped from around 47 (indicating contraction) to 54. Employment also increased. While not included in this chart package, another index tracking services (not manufacturing) shows orders and employment remain above 50 but less robust. So there are mixed signals. Regarding consumer confidence (University of Michigan data), we pay close attention to this. Consumers are not happy. You can talk all you want about GDP growth, or even cite the latest jobs report as proof of everything being fine, but consumers aren't buying it. Most indicators are trending downward, and I believe the University of Michigan index paints the bleakest (most pessimistic) picture of consumer sentiment. Much of the fear stems from employment and affordability. Even with marginal changes, consumers remain unhappy. Here's one reason: look at last year's employment revisions. This week we got the data—last year's employment was revised down by 861,000. Think about it—that's roughly 75,000 to 80,000 fewer jobs per month. And many of the original reports at the time were even lower than that, meaning they should have been negative. Last year was exceptionally weak for employment. It's understandable that consumers fear for the sustainability of their jobs. But there was good news in the last report too. We've been tracking the 16-24 age group in "In the Know" for a long time because that's where unemployment rose most sharply, once exceeding 12%. Now you can see it's fallen to 10% 
below. How did this happen? It could be improved employment, or it could be that AI has become so powerful that individuals can now directly start their own businesses. We believe significant entrepreneurial activity is occurring. If you look at new business startup data (which we'll show next time), it's growing well. When people get laid off or can't find entry-level jobs, they might become consultants while building their own businesses. I truly believe we'll see an explosion of entrepreneurial activity. We saw a chart today: When CEOs were asked, "How many of you save over 8 hours weekly thanks to AI?" about 43% said yes. But when workers were asked, only 5% agreed. This might be because workers use AI to boost efficiency and then enjoy the free time. But if there's a surge in entrepreneurial activity, more CEOs will seek these efficiency gains. This is another reason we expect widespread AI adoption to significantly boost productivity. Several charts further illustrate consumer unease. Savings rates are low, with some households living paycheck to paycheck, unable to save due to affordability crises (especially housing). Their auto loan delinquency rates are rising. Subprime loans (purple line) are elevated relative to 2008-2009 levels. In 
2008-2009, people prioritized defaulting on mortgages before auto loans because Uber or Lyft didn't exist. Today's landscape is different, which may explain why auto loan defaults are surging like this amid so-called growth conditions. Additionally, substantial tax refunds begin rolling out this week. We anticipate a surge around the end of March 
end of the month, putting money in the pockets of those living paycheck to paycheck. They might increase their savings rate or enjoy life a little more. Existing home sales are a shocking statistic. I digest economic data daily and rarely feel surprised, but seeing this figure fall instead of rise after mortgage rates dropped 90 basis points—even returning to lows—astonished me. As I mentioned earlier, prices continue to decline month-over-month, with year-over-year growth now at just 0.9%. This is intriguing—the market still lacks sufficient confidence to buy, or perhaps rates aren't low enough, or prices aren't low enough. Builders now have incentive to cut prices and continue subsidizing mortgage rates to clear inventory. Inventory not clearing is another reason prices will fall. Distorted Government Data and Macro Implications Before leaving economic indicators, regarding the downward revision of employment data, I must note: how chaotic, inaccurate, and flawed government statistics are. They were born in the industrial age, while we now live not just in the digital age, but the AI era. Change is happening too fast, and due to indexation structures, they can't keep up. Apologies if this sounds a bit esoteric, but if employment is actually much lower than initially reported, what does that mean for GDP accounts? GDP accounts are more accurate than employment metrics because GDP 
has another side called Gross National Income (GNI). This is quite accurate in terms of money changing hands, sales reports, and wage reports—all sourced from tax records. While statistical discrepancies exist, if fewer people are employed, it implies productivity is underestimated, meaning actual GDP growth is underestimated, and most critically—it means inflation is severely overestimated. We believe actual inflation likely aligns more with Truflation data (below 1%) than government statistics indicate. Market Indicators: Gold, Bitcoin, and the Crypto Ecosystem Now, looking at market indicators. We're watching the S&P 500 relative to gold. This was a significant warning signal in the 1970s (from 1966 to 1982 
, the S&P essentially treaded water). We certainly don't want to enter such a period, nor do we believe we will. But even as the Dow hits new highs, seeing this ratio decline is somewhat unsettling. Now look at the S&P 500 relative to oil prices. In the 1970s, the S&P's performance against oil mirrored its performance against gold. Now it's the exact opposite. This matters more because falling oil prices effectively reduce taxes for consumers and businesses. We welcome this divergence. And as I mentioned earlier, gold has been a bit "over its skis." Look at gold relative to M2—it's never been this high, surpassing even late 1970s levels (when inflation hit double digits) and the Great Depression. We must acknowledge gold has significantly outperformed Bitcoin. Bitcoin has, in a sense, been caught up in a "risk-off" dynamic where certain sectors experience wholesale sell-offs—a "sell first, ask questions later" mentality. This has happened in SaaS, wealth management, and truck brokerage. This algorithmic selling has certainly hurt Bitcoin, as many don't view it as a store of value or safe haven during turmoil the way they do gold. We don't understand this because we believe gold's supply growth is accelerating, while Bitcoin's supply growth cannot accelerate. We've discussed quantum computing and various concerns within the Bitcoin community, especially with ETFs bringing in new holders—potentially more vulnerable ones. When faced with risk, they sell first. However, you can see Bitcoin's uptrend—higher highs and higher lows— - remains intact. Although we dipped below a certain level in 2024, the overall upward trend holds. Technical analysts are now studying charts, and you'll notice our current position mirrors the 2017 peak, which serves as a significant marker. Support also exists in the 20-22-23k range, where Bitcoin previously acted as a refuge for those worried about regional bank crises escalating into a financial crisis with counterparty risk like that of 2008-2009. 
Bitcoin won't experience that kind of crisis. If you want to hedge counterparty risk, I encourage self-custody, because ETFs now dominate ownership. This period is agonizing—I dislike these times because I worry about our clients. But we use these periods to buy into crypto. This is when you see everyone's fear. I recently joined a company called LayerZero as an advisor. From a decentralized finance (DeFi) perspective, they're attempting to "go back to the future"—not by compromising through Layer 2 solutions like Ethereum, but by building an ecosystem for this new era (the era of agent-based AI). This era will see massive machine-to-machine (M2M) transactions requiring processing of 2 to 4 million transactions per second. 
Ethereum can only handle 13, Solana maybe 2,000. So during these tough times, the builders in this community are grinding away. Our research uncovered these new approaches as people began discussing them more within the context of the existing DeFi ecosystem. Conclusion: Comparison with the Dot-Com Bubble Finally, I want to summarize. Unlike the tech and telecom bubbles, the opportunities today are real. During that bubble, people were wildly speculative. Now, they're scared to death. As a portfolio manager in innovation, I prefer this current climate of fear and "climbing the wall of worry" over the excessive speculation of the tech bubble era. Some might argue AI is experiencing a bubble, which contributes to market anxiety. But we disagree. All our research indicates we're at a stage equivalent to 1996 
Of course, it later accelerated into hypergrowth and became frenetic. But back in 1996, Fed Chairman Greenspan spoke of an "irrational exuberance." That scared people because they thought he'd tighten policy to curb speculation, but he actually said he wouldn't—he'd let the market run its course. Because that's what happened next, we all learned valuable lessons. Today's markets carry these "scars" because those who lived through the tech bubble are now our industry's veterans. They possess that scar tissue or muscle memory. As the most seasoned investors, they say, "I must shield my company from this risk." I believe this mindset perpetuates fear and sustains that "wall of worry." Market volatility can be uncomfortable. But just like last April, these moments can be crucial opportunities to invest in "the next big thing." We're primed for prime time. We can see AI's explosion and feel the ground shifting beneath our feet. I'm now seeing and hearing CEOs say, "Oh my God, we have to do something about this." So I believe momentum will keep building. The most important thing is to be on the right side of the transformation. Thank you all, and have a great long weekend. 1.2%. This is a chart many Keynesian economists have been watching closely, expecting unit labor cost growth to accelerate or remain within the 5% to 7% range. 10%, whereas in the late 70s and early 80s it was closer to a quarter. Back then, union leaders were the driving force behind substantial increases in wages per hour worked. ; new home price inflation (i.e., price inflation tied to new home sales) remains negative; and rents are starting to decline. end of the month, putting money in the pockets of those living paycheck to paycheck. They might increase their savings rate or enjoy life a little more. Existing home sales are a shocking statistic. This period is agonizing—I dislike these times because I worry about our clients. But we use these periods to buy into crypto. This is when you see everyone's fear. I recently joined a company called LayerZero as an advisor. Ethereum can only handle 13, Solana maybe 2,000. So during these tough times, the builders in this community are grinding away. Our research uncovered these new approaches as people began discussing them more within the context of the existing DeFi ecosystem. Conclusion: Comparison with the Dot-Com Bubble Finally, I want to summarize. Unlike the tech and telecom bubbles, the opportunities today are real. During that bubble, people were wildly speculative. Now, they're scared to death. As a portfolio manager in innovation, I prefer this current climate of fear and "climbing the wall of worry" over the excessive speculation of the tech bubble era. Some might argue AI is experiencing a bubble, which contributes to market anxiety. But we disagree. All our research indicates we're at a stage equivalent to 1996 
Of course, it later accelerated into hypergrowth and became frenetic. But back in 1996, Fed Chairman Greenspan spoke of an "irrational exuberance." That scared people because they thought he'd tighten policy to curb speculation, but he actually said he wouldn't—he'd let the market run its course. Because that's what happened next, we all learned valuable lessons. Today's markets carry these "scars" because those who lived through the tech bubble are now our industry's veterans. They possess that scar tissue or muscle memory. As the most seasoned investors, they say, "I must shield my company from this risk." I believe this mindset perpetuates fear and sustains that "wall of worry." Market volatility can be uncomfortable. But just like last April, these moments can be crucial opportunities to invest in "the next big thing." We're primed for prime time. We can see AI's explosion and feel the ground shifting beneath our feet. I'm now seeing and hearing CEOs say, "Oh my God, we have to do something about this." So I believe momentum will keep building. The most important thing is to be on the right side of the transformation. Thank you all, and have a great long weekend. If we experience negative inflation (which I believe we will) while real GDP 
rapidly rising, the current Fed administration might tighten policy—that would be a grave mistake. This Fed believes "growth causes inflation." Kevin Warsh is correct: growth does not cause inflation. In fact, it accelerates productivity growth, thereby suppressing inflation. This is the 
story behind the miraculous stock markets of the 1980s and 1990s: real growth accelerated, inflation declined, and productivity was the primary driver. Productivity and wage productivity measure output per hour worked, while unit labor costs represent wages adjusted for productivity. If you examine this chart, our current unit labor cost growth rate is approximately 
1.2%. This is a chart many Keynesian economists have been watching closely, expecting unit labor cost growth to accelerate or remain within the 5% to 7% range. They didn't get productivity right—it was stronger than anticipated; nor did they get wages right—the growth rate was lower than expected. They learned their lesson from the 1960s and 1970s 
. Back then, unit labor costs (as shown in the chart) soared into double digits as workers demanded higher wage increases to offset skyrocketing food and energy prices. Given the supply shocks during the COVID-19 pandemic, they were prepared for the same outcome this time. But as you can see, that hasn't happened. Any Keynesian economist must look at this and say, "This time is different." Despite solid real GDP growth and low unemployment in the aggregate data, we haven't seen unit labor costs rebound. One reason is that workers aren't making the same tough demands as in the 1970s. Another is falling oil prices, which marginally help budgets. A third reason, I believe, is that unionized workers now make up less than 10% of total employment 
10%, whereas in the late 70s and early 80s it was closer to a quarter. Back then, union leaders were the driving force behind substantial increases in wages per hour worked. To be clear: if our prediction of an impending economic boom proves correct, and if opportunities emerge as we anticipate with real growth accelerating, we don't believe unions will be necessary. If companies leverage these new technologies—AI, robotics, and so forth—wage growth will accelerate for a sound reason: it will reflect productivity gains for all workers. ; new home price inflation (i.e., price inflation tied to new home sales) remains negative; and rents are starting to decline. These data points take a long time to feed into the government's CPI index. Therefore, we believe these price pressures will exert downward pressure on CPI in the coming years. We are quite confident about this. Now consider oil prices, which show double-digit year-over-year declines. This typically occurs during economic recessions. It shows inflation is collapsing after years of hovering between 2% and 3%. The current reading is approximately 0.7% year-over-year. This is a real-time indicator tracking 10,000 goods and services. But if you look at today's food prices relative to pre-pandemic levels, they're still about 32% higher. This essentially indicates manufacturing has been in pain, even if we weren't technically in a recession like when the index is negative—it was a rolling recession. below. How did this happen? It could be improved employment, or it could be that AI has become so powerful that individuals can now directly start their own businesses. This might be because workers use AI to boost efficiency and then enjoy the free time. But if there's a surge in entrepreneurial activity, more CEOs will seek these efficiency gains. This is another reason we expect widespread AI adoption to significantly boost productivity. Several charts further illustrate consumer unease. Subprime loans (purple line) are elevated relative to 2008-2009 levels. In 
2008-2009, people prioritized defaulting on mortgages before auto loans because Uber or Lyft didn't exist. Today's landscape is different, which may explain why auto loan defaults are surging like this amid so-called growth conditions. Additionally, substantial tax refunds begin rolling out this week. We anticipate a surge around the end of March 
end of the month, putting money in the pockets of those living paycheck to paycheck. They might increase their savings rate or enjoy life a little more. Existing home sales are a shocking statistic. I digest economic data daily and rarely feel surprised, but seeing this figure fall instead of rise after mortgage rates dropped 90 basis points—even returning to lows—astonished me. As I mentioned earlier, prices continue to decline month-over-month, with year-over-year growth now at just 0.9%. This is intriguing—the market still lacks sufficient confidence to buy, or perhaps rates aren't low enough, or prices aren't low enough. Builders now have incentive to cut prices and continue subsidizing mortgage rates to clear inventory. Inventory not clearing is another reason prices will fall. They were born in the industrial age, while we now live not just in the digital age, but the AI era. Change is happening too fast, and due to indexation structures, they can't keep up. Apologies if this sounds a bit esoteric, but if employment is actually much lower than initially reported, what does that mean for GDP accounts? GDP accounts are more accurate than employment metrics because GDP 
has another side called Gross National Income (GNI). This is quite accurate in terms of money changing hands, sales reports, and wage reports—all sourced from tax records. While statistical discrepancies exist, if fewer people are employed, it implies productivity is underestimated, meaning actual GDP growth is underestimated, and most critically—it means inflation is severely overestimated. We believe actual inflation likely aligns more with Truflation data (below 1%) than government statistics indicate. This was a significant warning signal in the 1970s (from 1966 to 1982 
, the S&P essentially treaded water). We certainly don't want to enter such a period, nor do we believe we will. But even as the Dow hits new highs, seeing this ratio decline is somewhat unsettling. Now look at the S&P 500 relative to oil prices. In the 1970s, the S&P's performance against oil mirrored its performance against gold. Now it's the exact opposite. This matters more because falling oil prices effectively reduce taxes for consumers and businesses. We welcome this divergence. And as I mentioned earlier, gold has been a bit "over its skis." Look at gold relative to M2—it's never been this high, surpassing even late 1970s levels (when inflation hit double digits) and the Great Depression. We must acknowledge gold has significantly outperformed Bitcoin. Bitcoin has, in a sense, been caught up in a "risk-off" dynamic where certain sectors experience wholesale sell-offs—a "sell first, ask questions later" mentality. This has happened in SaaS, wealth management, and truck brokerage. This algorithmic selling has certainly hurt Bitcoin, as many don't view it as a store of value or safe haven during turmoil the way they do gold. We don't understand this because we believe gold's supply growth is accelerating, while Bitcoin's supply growth cannot accelerate. We've discussed quantum computing and various concerns within the Bitcoin community, especially with ETFs bringing in new holders—potentially more vulnerable ones. When faced with risk, they sell first. However, you can see Bitcoin's uptrend—higher highs and higher lows— - remains intact. Although we dipped below a certain level in 2024, the overall upward trend holds. Technical analysts are now studying charts, and you'll notice our current position mirrors the 2017 peak, which serves as a significant marker. Support also exists in the 20-22-23k range, where Bitcoin previously acted as a refuge for those worried about regional bank crises escalating into a financial crisis with counterparty risk like that of 2008-2009. 
Bitcoin won't experience that kind of crisis. If you want to hedge counterparty risk, I encourage self-custody, because ETFs now dominate ownership. This period is agonizing—I dislike these times because I worry about our clients. But we use these periods to buy into crypto. This is when you see everyone's fear. I recently joined a company called LayerZero as an advisor. From a decentralized finance (DeFi) perspective, they're attempting to "go back to the future"—not by compromising through Layer 2 solutions like Ethereum, but by building an ecosystem for this new era (the era of agent-based AI). This era will see massive machine-to-machine (M2M) transactions requiring processing of 2 to 4 million transactions per second. 
Ethereum can only handle 13, Solana maybe 2,000. So during these tough times, the builders in this community are grinding away. Our research uncovered these new approaches as people began discussing them more within the context of the existing DeFi ecosystem. Conclusion: Comparison with the Dot-Com Bubble Finally, I want to summarize. Unlike the tech and telecom bubbles, the opportunities today are real. During that bubble, people were wildly speculative. Now, they're scared to death. As a portfolio manager in innovation, I prefer this current climate of fear and "climbing the wall of worry" over the excessive speculation of the tech bubble era. Some might argue AI is experiencing a bubble, which contributes to market anxiety. But we disagree. All our research indicates we're at a stage equivalent to 1996 
Of course, it later accelerated into hypergrowth and became frenetic. But back in 1996, Fed Chairman Greenspan spoke of an "irrational exuberance." That scared people because they thought he'd tighten policy to curb speculation, but he actually said he wouldn't—he'd let the market run its course. Because that's what happened next, we all learned valuable lessons. Today's markets carry these "scars" because those who lived through the tech bubble are now our industry's veterans. They possess that scar tissue or muscle memory. As the most seasoned investors, they say, "I must shield my company from this risk." I believe this mindset perpetuates fear and sustains that "wall of worry." Market volatility can be uncomfortable. But just like last April, these moments can be crucial opportunities to invest in "the next big thing." We're primed for prime time. We can see AI's explosion and feel the ground shifting beneath our feet. I'm now seeing and hearing CEOs say, "Oh my God, we have to do something about this." So I believe momentum will keep building. The most important thing is to be on the right side of the transformation. Thank you all, and have a great long weekend. Amid escalating debates over AI capital expenditures, Cathie Wood attributes the U.S. stock market's "sharp swings" to a chain reaction of algorithmic selling. On February 14 local time, ARK Invest CEO and CIO Cathie Wood stated in her February episode of the video series "ITK" that recent market volatility is largely driven by algorithmic trading rather than fundamental shifts of comparable magnitude. She opened the program by stating: "Most of this volatility is algorithmically 'manufactured.' Algorithms don't do research like we do." Wood noted that such volatility can "scare people," but it also creates pricing inefficiencies. "During the tariff turmoil last April, many panicked. Those who sold then spent the next year regretting it." She described the current market as "climbing the wall of worry," noting such markets often prove stronger. Why do algorithms "create" volatility? The "algorithms" Wood refers to don't fundamentally assess corporate cash flows or competitive landscapes; instead, they mechanically adjust risk exposures based on predefined rules. She summarized recent market behavior succinctly: "Sell first, ask questions later." From a trading mechanism perspective, algorithmic strategies often trigger based on price trends, volatility, correlations, or position risk budgets: When prices fall or volatility rises, models automatically reduce exposure to risk assets to meet predetermined drawdown/volatility targets. This reduction itself further amplifies volatility and correlations, triggering more model-driven selling and creating a "feedback loop." In crowded sectors with highly homogeneous positions, this chain reaction easily drags down both "good companies" and "bad companies" together—as she put it, "throwing the baby out with the bathwater." She also pointed to another amplifier: the rising dominance of technical analysis-driven trading psychology. "Nowadays, many people rely solely on technical analysis." In her view, the more traders fixate on the same moving average or "key level," the more likely they are to trigger stampede-like, directional trading. "Structural Transformation" Beyond Algorithmic Understanding Regarding the recent volatility in tech stocks, particularly the software sector, Wood believes the market is undergoing a technological shift from a "one-size-fits-all" SaaS model toward highly customized AI agent platforms. Traditional SaaS facing pressure is inevitable in this process, but the market has overreacted. "Anyone selling at this moment will regret it," Wood bluntly stated in the video. "Most of this volatility is algorithm-driven. Algorithms aren't doing the research we're doing, because this is the biggest opportunity of our lifetime." She detailed how this mechanism fails: When the market senses slowing growth in the SaaS sector, algorithmic trading tends to execute indiscriminate sell orders. Machines cannot distinguish which companies are successfully transitioning into AI platforms and which will be left behind. This mispricing, caused by algorithms' lack of deep fundamental research, presents an opportunity for active investors. "That's why we concentrate our positions in stocks we believe in most. The market has given us this opportunity." Wood stated that the current market environment is climbing a "wall of worry," a hallmark of robust bull markets. Giant companies "burning cash" is necessary: This is 1996, not 1999. Widespread market concerns that the aggressive capital expenditures of the "Tech Seven" (Mag 7) will erode cash flow have prompted some investors traditionally focused on free cash flow to reduce their positions recently. Wood holds the exact opposite view. She drew parallels to the dot-com bubble era, noting that the current situation resembles 1996— - the stage when the internet revolution was just beginning. "If you lived through the tech and telecom bubble, the environment today is far healthier than it was then." She used a vivid comparison to illustrate the soundness of current market sentiment: At the peak of the dot-com bubble, Jeff Bezos could say, "We'll lose more money to invest aggressively," and Amazon's stock would surge 10% to 15%. But today, the situation is completely reversed. When the 'Tech Six' (Mag 6) announced increased capital expenditures, the market punished them instead, causing their stock prices to fall rather than rise. Wood believes this indicates investors are not caught up in irrational exuberance but are instead filled with fear and skepticism. The market is climbing a 'wall of worry,' which typically forms the strongest foundation for long-term bull markets rather than signaling an impending bubble burst. 
Today's investors carry the "scarred memory" of the 2000 bubble burst, making them extremely cautious toward new technologies. "We believe Google, Meta, Microsoft, and Amazon should spend aggressively because this is the biggest opportunity of our lifetime," Wood countered the market's short-sightedness. "The question is: as we evolve toward agent AI and chatbots, will this steal time away from traditional social media? From a shopping perspective, will our smart agents handle everything? We do need to monitor shifts in market share, but that's precisely where the opportunity lies." Productivity Shock May Pull Inflation Lower Wood extends AI's impact to the macro level: rising productivity could alter the traditional narrative that "growth inevitably fuels inflation." She notes that productivity gains will reduce fiscal deficits as a percentage of GDP, asserting that the U.S. could move toward a surplus by the end of the current presidential term (citing late 2028 to early 2029 as the timeline). She even projected "global real GDP growth of 7%-8% by the decade's end," calling it "potentially conservative." She repeatedly emphasized one conclusion: "Growth does not equal inflation." Within her framework, AI-driven real growth is more likely to suppress inflation through productivity gains rather than fuel it. She also noted that a dollar rebound would become a "powerful anti-inflationary force." Regarding inflation metrics, she highlighted the "most important page": Truflation's real-time inflation indicator shows inflation "breaking down," with her reading at approximately 0.7% year-over-year. She further addressed marginal shifts in housing and energy: " Existing home price inflation has fallen below 1%," new home price inflation remains negative, and rents are beginning to decline; oil prices show a "double-digit year-over-year drop," which she described as equivalent to a "tax cut" for consumers and businesses. Labor Market Pain and Entrepreneurial Wave Addressing the current low consumer confidence, Wood acknowledged that consumers "are not happy," primarily due to genuine weakness in the labor market and a crisis in housing affordability. "Last year's employment figures were revised downward by 861,000, which roughly equates to a monthly loss of 75,000 jobs," Wood noted, explaining why consumer sentiment diverged from GDP data. However, she found a silver lining in youth unemployment figures. While the unemployment rate for those aged 16-24 had surged, it had recently fallen below 10%. Wood believes this represents not merely job recovery, but likely an "Entrepreneurial Explosion" fueled by AI. "AI has become so powerful that individuals can now directly go out and build businesses." She predicts that as AI tools become widespread, a surge of highly efficient startups driven by individuals or small teams will emerge, serving as another crucial engine for productivity growth. The question is: As we evolve toward agentic AI and chatbots, will this cannibalize time spent on traditional social media? From a shopping perspective, will our smart agents handle everything online, meaning Amazon's cut won't be as significant as it has been recently? We'll wait and see, and we'll certainly monitor market share shifts closely—just as we accurately predicted the SaaS landscape's transformation. Fiscal Policy: Deficits and GDP Outlook Alright, let's start with the charts. I'd characterize the current environment as macroeconomic conditions akin to trade turmoil periods, presenting massive opportunities—as I mentioned earlier, we'll definitely leverage this volatility. Our long-term performance is built precisely on such decisions. Let's examine the charts. The budget deficit as a percentage of GDP briefly dipped below 5%, but this proved fleeting until first-quarter GDP data and forecasts were revised downward. We're now approaching the "4% range." Treasury Secretary Bessent targets 3%. In fact, we are increasingly confident that by the end of this presidential term—late 2028 or early 2029—we will achieve a fiscal surplus. This stems from productivity growth far exceeding expectations. Some examples are downright staggering, like Palantir. Its U.S. commercial revenue surged 142%, while I believe Palantir actually reduced its sales force slightly. This productivity surge is astonishing—at least from a sales perspective, exceeding 100%, even surpassing 140%. I believe this is the mindset we all need to cultivate, a "muscle" we must exercise when discussing how the world and companies operate. Elon Musk sets wildly ambitious targets, and I think that's the right mindset. Companies lacking this mindset will lose opportunities to more agile competitors. We've proposed that by the end of this decade, global real GDP growth could reach 7% to 8%. Many scoffed at this. But given what we're witnessing, this forecast might even be too conservative. It could mean a significant reduction in the deficit as a percentage of GDP. Barring some foolish policies, we'll shift into surplus. The COVID pandemic was a massive panic that deeply unsettled bond markets and investors. Now we're correcting course, hoping to learn lessons and ultimately achieve surpluses like in the 90s—when the last small tech revolution (the internet) occurred. Trade Deficits and the Dollar's Path Next is another deficit. In the 70s and 80s 
, bond markets were highly vigilant about the "twin deficits": the federal budget deficit and the trade deficit. You can see the trade deficit during and after the pandemic: imports surged, plunging us into a massive deficit. As the chart shows, this is also changing. Many in the foreign exchange market use the trade deficit to gauge the dollar's direction. If we import far more than we export, concerns arise that the dollar will weaken. This creates a pivot point in the market. You can see we've corrected this. Of course, imports plummeted earlier due to tariffs and anticipated tariff policies (as businesses rushed to import ahead of time). If our judgment was correct, we're now seeing the opposite effect. The limited extent of the deficit a few months ago surprised me. If we are correct—that the U.S. will exceed expectations in real GDP growth and that U.S. capital returns will rise due to tax cuts, deregulation, and tariffs—then we may see more import growth. Not because of tariffs, of course, but due to the first two factors. We will continue to run deficits. However, we have never truly worried about the trade deficit. Because when you consider the full picture, the flip side of the trade balance (goods and services) is a capital surplus. Given the U.S.'s generally favorable environment for business and capital, we attract funds from the rest of the world. I know people worry about twin deficits, so I specifically address this. Regarding the trade deficit, I've already explained why we aren't concerned. Regarding the federal deficit, we've seen a significant improvement in the deficit as a percentage of GDP. I want to describe the dollar's trajectory. I know this chart has been shown before, and it represents another external concern. You'll hear a lot about the "end of American exceptionalism," which can mean different things to different people. But from an economic standpoint, I would say it hasn't ended. In fact, due to this technology revolution led by the U.S. and China, we may see an explosion of U.S. economic activity. So I genuinely believe the dollar will turn and move in the other direction. Of course, the dollar's decline is largely political. I think countries are diversifying their investments away from the dollar toward gold and other currencies, but we believe this will change. Given the gold price trend, it has reached extremes. But look at what's happening with the dollar. If you look at this chart and zoom out, this isn't a dollar collapse. If you're a technical analyst, you'd say, "Hey, the dollar has stabilized at the expected support level," meaning the previous peak (as shown by the black line). If the dollar rises, one point I'm watching is: a rising dollar acts as a powerful anti-inflationary force. We genuinely believe the dollar will strengthen, leading to lower-than-expected inflation. Inflation, Money Supply, and Fed Policy Here we see CPI (green line, year-over-year percentage change) versus M2. We're still recovering from negative M2 growth, but I believe we've stabilized within the 5% range. As I mentioned earlier, we also see money velocity beginning to level off or decline. A decline or stabilization in velocity would partially unwind inflation expectations tied to money growth. If you look at the green line, it appears stuck in the 2% to 3% range, where it has been for the past several years. We expect it to decline significantly this year. I've been saying this for a long time: I didn't anticipate supply shocks would make inflation so sticky, nor did I expect them to persist for three years—that was unforeseen. But considering the turbulent context at the time, maintaining it within the 2% to 3% range was actually quite good. This may suggest or validate our view that we'll see inflation decline. To help understand monetary policy, here's the short end of the yield curve: the 2-year Treasury yield versus the 3-month Treasury yield. You can see it remains in negative territory, indicating that marginally, the Fed isn't accommodative—it's even a bit too tight. Based on the 10-year versus 2-year Treasury yield metric, it's in positive territory, but you can see it's sloping downward. If the Fed were to aggressively ease policy, you'd expect this line to continue rising. If we start seeing negative inflation data (which I believe we will), the Fed might aggressively ease. We may have to wait until Kevin Warsh takes over before we see negative inflation in the year-over-year data. We know Kevin Warsh is a very disciplined economist from a monetary perspective. Hearing him say, "This AI revolution could accelerate growth in many sectors of the economy, and as monetary authorities, we should accommodate this real growth as long as it doesn't trigger inflation," is very reassuring. If we experience negative inflation (which I believe we will) while real GDP 
rapidly rising, the current Fed administration might tighten policy—that would be a grave mistake. This Fed believes "growth causes inflation." Kevin Warsh is correct: growth does not cause inflation. In fact, it accelerates productivity growth, thereby suppressing inflation. This is the 
story behind the miraculous stock markets of the 1980s and 1990s: real growth accelerated, inflation declined, and productivity was the primary driver. Productivity and wage productivity measure output per hour worked, while unit labor costs represent wages adjusted for productivity. If you examine this chart, our current unit labor cost growth rate is approximately 
1.2%. This is a chart many Keynesian economists have been watching closely, expecting unit labor cost growth to accelerate or remain within the 5% to 7% range. They didn't get productivity right—it was stronger than anticipated; nor did they get wages right—the growth rate was lower than expected. They learned their lesson from the 1960s and 1970s 
. Back then, unit labor costs (as shown in the chart) soared into double digits as workers demanded higher wage increases to offset skyrocketing food and energy prices. Given the supply shocks during the COVID-19 pandemic, they were prepared for the same outcome this time. But as you can see, that hasn't happened. Any Keynesian economist must look at this and say, "This time is different." Despite solid real GDP growth and low unemployment in the aggregate data, we haven't seen unit labor costs rebound. One reason is that workers aren't making the same tough demands as in the 1970s. Another is falling oil prices, which marginally help budgets. A third reason, I believe, is that unionized workers now make up less than 10% of total employment 
10%, whereas in the late 70s and early 80s it was closer to a quarter. Back then, union leaders were the driving force behind substantial increases in wages per hour worked. To be clear: if our prediction of an impending economic boom proves correct, and if opportunities emerge as we anticipate with real growth accelerating, we don't believe unions will be necessary. If companies leverage these new technologies—AI, robotics, and so forth—wage growth will accelerate for a sound reason: it will reflect productivity gains for all workers. Price Trends: Real Estate, Oil, and Inflation Data Let's discuss the next reason we believe inflation won't exceed expectations. Consider real estate prices: the green line showing existing home price inflation has fallen below 1%. 
; new home price inflation (i.e., price inflation tied to new home sales) remains negative; and rents are starting to decline. These data points take a long time to feed into the government's CPI index. Therefore, we believe these price pressures will exert downward pressure on CPI in the coming years. We are quite confident about this. Now consider oil prices, which show double-digit year-over-year declines. This typically occurs during economic recessions. But we now observe this as a global phenomenon. Of course, Saudi Arabia in the Middle East is a swing factor, consistently increasing supply. I believe this may relate to political dynamics arising from negotiations between the Trump administration and Saudi Arabia on various matters, including defense and other programs. Here we separate core CPI (purple) from core PPI (green). If you look closely, you'll see PPI inflation has already outpaced CPI inflation. So those holding consumer goods company stocks in the market may hear more about margin pressures—keep an eye on that. This is the most important slide: Truflation (Real Inflation). It shows inflation is collapsing after years of hovering between 2% and 3%. The current reading is approximately 0.7% year-over-year. This is a real-time indicator tracking 10,000 goods and services. You can see it captured inflation peaks better than CPI—it once approached 12% while CPI was at 9%. One thing we see in the real-time data is that food price inflation is declining. We're seeing deflation in eggs and some food items that caused pain during the pandemic, which is positive. But if you look at today's food prices relative to pre-pandemic levels, they're still about 32% higher. So I believe we'll see food price inflation fall further, which is another reason inflation could turn negative. Real Economy and Consumer Sentiment Regarding real activity, this is the Manufacturing ISM 
(Purchasing Managers' Index). You can see a solid rebound here. We've described the past three years as a "rolling recession." If you look at housing and all U.S. manufacturing, they've been below 50. This essentially indicates manufacturing has been in pain, even if we weren't technically in a recession like when the index is negative—it was a rolling recession. Now, this appears to be the first sign of change. The biggest shift in this PMI is orders. The new orders index jumped from around 47 (indicating contraction) to 54. Employment also increased. While not included in this chart package, another index tracking services (not manufacturing) shows orders and employment remain above 50 but less robust. So there are mixed signals. Regarding consumer confidence (University of Michigan data), we pay close attention to this. Consumers are not happy. You can talk all you want about GDP growth, or even cite the latest jobs report as proof of everything being fine, but consumers aren't buying it. Most indicators are trending downward, and I believe the University of Michigan index paints the bleakest (most pessimistic) picture of consumer sentiment. Much of the fear stems from employment and affordability. Even with marginal changes, consumers remain unhappy. Here's one reason: look at last year's employment revisions. This week we got the data—last year's employment was revised down by 861,000. Think about it—that's roughly 75,000 to 80,000 fewer jobs per month. And many of the original reports at the time were even lower than that, meaning they should have been negative. Last year was exceptionally weak for employment. It's understandable that consumers fear for the sustainability of their jobs. But there was good news in the last report too. We've been tracking the 16-24 age group in "In the Know" for a long time because that's where unemployment rose most sharply, once exceeding 12%. Now you can see it's fallen to 10% 
below. How did this happen? It could be improved employment, or it could be that AI has become so powerful that individuals can now directly start their own businesses. We believe significant entrepreneurial activity is occurring. If you look at new business startup data (which we'll show next time), it's growing well. When people get laid off or can't find entry-level jobs, they might become consultants while building their own businesses. I truly believe we'll see an explosion of entrepreneurial activity. We saw a chart today: When CEOs were asked, "How many of you save over 8 hours weekly thanks to AI?" about 43% said yes. But when workers were asked, only 5% agreed. This might be because workers use AI to boost efficiency and then enjoy the free time. But if there's a surge in entrepreneurial activity, more CEOs will seek these efficiency gains. This is another reason we expect widespread AI adoption to significantly boost productivity. Several charts further illustrate consumer unease. Savings rates are low, with some households living paycheck to paycheck, unable to save due to affordability crises (especially housing). Their auto loan delinquency rates are rising. Subprime loans (purple line) are elevated relative to 2008-2009 levels. In 
2008-2009, people prioritized defaulting on mortgages before auto loans because Uber or Lyft didn't exist. Today's landscape is different, which may explain why auto loan defaults are surging like this amid so-called growth conditions. Additionally, substantial tax refunds begin rolling out this week. We anticipate a surge around the end of March 
end of the month, putting money in the pockets of those living paycheck to paycheck. They might increase their savings rate or enjoy life a little more. Existing home sales are a shocking statistic. I digest economic data daily and rarely feel surprised, but seeing this figure fall instead of rise after mortgage rates dropped 90 basis points—even returning to lows—astonished me. As I mentioned earlier, prices continue to decline month-over-month, with year-over-year growth now at just 0.9%. This is intriguing—the market still lacks sufficient confidence to buy, or perhaps rates aren't low enough, or prices aren't low enough. Builders now have incentive to cut prices and continue subsidizing mortgage rates to clear inventory. Inventory not clearing is another reason prices will fall. Distorted Government Data and Macro Implications Before leaving economic indicators, regarding the downward revision of employment data, I must note: how chaotic, inaccurate, and flawed government statistics are. They were born in the industrial age, while we now live not just in the digital age, but the AI era. Change is happening too fast, and due to indexation structures, they can't keep up. Apologies if this sounds a bit esoteric, but if employment is actually much lower than initially reported, what does that mean for GDP accounts? GDP accounts are more accurate than employment metrics because GDP 
has another side called Gross National Income (GNI). This is quite accurate in terms of money changing hands, sales reports, and wage reports—all sourced from tax records. While statistical discrepancies exist, if fewer people are employed, it implies productivity is underestimated, meaning actual GDP growth is underestimated, and most critically—it means inflation is severely overestimated. We believe actual inflation likely aligns more with Truflation data (below 1%) than government statistics indicate. Market Indicators: Gold, Bitcoin, and the Crypto Ecosystem Now, looking at market indicators. We're watching the S&P 500 relative to gold. This was a significant warning signal in the 1970s (from 1966 to 1982 
, the S&P essentially treaded water). We certainly don't want to enter such a period, nor do we believe we will. But even as the Dow hits new highs, seeing this ratio decline is somewhat unsettling. Now look at the S&P 500 relative to oil prices. In the 1970s, the S&P's performance against oil mirrored its performance against gold. Now it's the exact opposite. This matters more because falling oil prices effectively reduce taxes for consumers and businesses. We welcome this divergence. And as I mentioned earlier, gold has been a bit "over its skis." Look at gold relative to M2—it's never been this high, surpassing even late 1970s levels (when inflation hit double digits) and the Great Depression. We must acknowledge gold has significantly outperformed Bitcoin. Bitcoin has, in a sense, been caught up in a "risk-off" dynamic where certain sectors experience wholesale sell-offs—a "sell first, ask questions later" mentality. This has happened in SaaS, wealth management, and truck brokerage. This algorithmic selling has certainly hurt Bitcoin, as many don't view it as a store of value or safe haven during turmoil the way they do gold. We don't understand this because we believe gold's supply growth is accelerating, while Bitcoin's supply growth cannot accelerate. We've discussed quantum computing and various concerns within the Bitcoin community, especially with ETFs bringing in new holders—potentially more vulnerable ones. When faced with risk, they sell first. However, you can see Bitcoin's uptrend—higher highs and higher lows— - remains intact. Although we dipped below a certain level in 2024, the overall upward trend holds. Technical analysts are now studying charts, and you'll notice our current position mirrors the 2017 peak, which serves as a significant marker. Support also exists in the 20-22-23k range, where Bitcoin previously acted as a refuge for those worried about regional bank crises escalating into a financial crisis with counterparty risk like that of 2008-2009. 
Bitcoin won't experience that kind of crisis. If you want to hedge counterparty risk, I encourage self-custody, because ETFs now dominate ownership. This period is agonizing—I dislike these times because I worry about our clients. But we use these periods to buy into crypto. This is when you see everyone's fear. I recently joined a company called LayerZero as an advisor. From a decentralized finance (DeFi) perspective, they're attempting to "go back to the future"—not by compromising through Layer 2 solutions like Ethereum, but by building an ecosystem for this new era (the era of agent-based AI). This era will see massive machine-to-machine (M2M) transactions requiring processing of 2 to 4 million transactions per second. 
Ethereum can only handle 13, Solana maybe 2,000. So during these tough times, the builders in this community are grinding away. Our research uncovered these new approaches as people began discussing them more within the context of the existing DeFi ecosystem. Conclusion: Comparison with the Dot-Com Bubble Finally, I want to summarize. Unlike the tech and telecom bubbles, the opportunities today are real. During that bubble, people were wildly speculative. Now, they're scared to death. As a portfolio manager in innovation, I prefer this current climate of fear and "climbing the wall of worry" over the excessive speculation of the tech bubble era. Some might argue AI is experiencing a bubble, which contributes to market anxiety. But we disagree. All our research indicates we're at a stage equivalent to 1996 
Of course, it later accelerated into hypergrowth and became frenetic. But back in 1996, Fed Chairman Greenspan spoke of an "irrational exuberance." That scared people because they thought he'd tighten policy to curb speculation, but he actually said he wouldn't—he'd let the market run its course. Because that's what happened next, we all learned valuable lessons. Today's markets carry these "scars" because those who lived through the tech bubble are now our industry's veterans. They possess that scar tissue or muscle memory. As the most seasoned investors, they say, "I must shield my company from this risk." I believe this mindset perpetuates fear and sustains that "wall of worry." Market volatility can be uncomfortable. But just like last April, these moments can be crucial opportunities to invest in "the next big thing." We're primed for prime time. We can see AI's explosion and feel the ground shifting beneath our feet. I'm now seeing and hearing CEOs say, "Oh my God, we have to do something about this." So I believe momentum will keep building. The most important thing is to be on the right side of the transformation. Thank you all, and have a great long weekend. Amid escalating debates over AI capital expenditures, Cathie Wood attributes the U.S. stock market's "sharp swings" to a chain reaction of algorithmic selling. On February 14 local time, ARK Invest CEO and CIO Cathie Wood stated in her February episode of the video series "ITK" that recent market volatility is largely driven by algorithmic trading rather than fundamental shifts of comparable magnitude. She opened the program by stating: "Most of this volatility is algorithmically 'manufactured.' Algorithms don't do research like we do." Wood noted that such volatility can "scare people," but it also creates pricing inefficiencies. "During the tariff turmoil last April, many panicked. Those who sold then spent the next year regretting it." She described the current market as "climbing the wall of worry," noting such markets often prove stronger. Why do algorithms "create" volatility? The "algorithms" Wood refers to don't fundamentally assess corporate cash flows or competitive landscapes; instead, they mechanically adjust risk exposures based on predefined rules. She summarized recent market behavior succinctly: "Sell first, ask questions later." From a trading mechanism perspective, algorithmic strategies often trigger based on price trends, volatility, correlations, or position risk budgets: When prices fall or volatility rises, models automatically reduce exposure to risk assets to meet predetermined drawdown/volatility targets. This reduction itself further amplifies volatility and correlations, triggering more model-driven selling and creating a "feedback loop." In crowded sectors with highly homogeneous positions, this chain reaction easily drags down both "good companies" and "bad companies" together—as she put it, "throwing the baby out with the bathwater." She also pointed to another amplifier: the rising dominance of technical analysis-driven trading psychology. "Nowadays, many people rely solely on technical analysis." In her view, the more traders fixate on the same moving average or "key level," the more likely they are to trigger stampede-like, directional trading. "Structural Transformation" Beyond Algorithmic Understanding Regarding the recent volatility in tech stocks, particularly the software sector, Wood believes the market is undergoing a technological shift from a "one-size-fits-all" SaaS model toward highly customized AI agent platforms. Traditional SaaS facing pressure is inevitable in this process, but the market has overreacted. "Anyone selling at this moment will regret it," Wood bluntly stated in the video. "Most of this volatility is algorithm-driven. Algorithms aren't doing the research we're doing, because this is the biggest opportunity of our lifetime." She detailed how this mechanism fails: When the market senses slowing growth in the SaaS sector, algorithmic trading tends to execute indiscriminate sell orders. Machines cannot distinguish which companies are successfully transitioning into AI platforms and which will be left behind. This mispricing, caused by algorithms' lack of deep fundamental research, presents an opportunity for active investors. "That's why we concentrate our positions in stocks we believe in most. The market has given us this opportunity." Wood stated that the current market environment is climbing a "wall of worry," a hallmark of robust bull markets. Giant companies "burning cash" is necessary: This is 1996, not 1999. Widespread market concerns that the aggressive capital expenditures of the "Tech Seven" (Mag 7) will erode cash flow have prompted some investors traditionally focused on free cash flow to reduce their positions recently. Wood holds the exact opposite view. She drew parallels to the dot-com bubble era, noting that the current situation resembles 1996— - the stage when the internet revolution was just beginning. "If you lived through the tech and telecom bubble, the environment today is far healthier than it was then." She used a vivid comparison to illustrate the soundness of current market sentiment: At the peak of the dot-com bubble, Jeff Bezos could say, "We'll lose more money to invest aggressively," and Amazon's stock would surge 10% to 15%. But today, the situation is completely reversed. When the 'Tech Six' (Mag 6) announced increased capital expenditures, the market punished them instead, causing their stock prices to fall rather than rise. Wood believes this indicates investors are not caught up in irrational exuberance but are instead filled with fear and skepticism. The market is climbing a 'wall of worry,' which typically forms the strongest foundation for long-term bull markets rather than signaling an impending bubble burst. 
Today's investors carry the "scarred memory" of the 2000 bubble burst, making them extremely cautious toward new technologies. "We believe Google, Meta, Microsoft, and Amazon should spend aggressively because this is the biggest opportunity of our lifetime," Wood countered the market's short-sightedness. "The question is: as we evolve toward agent AI and chatbots, will this steal time away from traditional social media? From a shopping perspective, will our smart agents handle everything? We do need to monitor shifts in market share, but that's precisely where the opportunity lies." Productivity Shock May Pull Inflation Lower Wood extends AI's impact to the macro level: rising productivity could alter the traditional narrative that "growth inevitably fuels inflation." She notes that productivity gains will reduce fiscal deficits as a percentage of GDP, asserting that the U.S. could move toward a surplus by the end of the current presidential term (citing late 2028 to early 2029 as the timeline). She even projected "global real GDP growth of 7%-8% by the decade's end," calling it "potentially conservative." She repeatedly emphasized one conclusion: "Growth does not equal inflation." Within her framework, AI-driven real growth is more likely to suppress inflation through productivity gains rather than fuel it. She also noted that a dollar rebound would become a "powerful anti-inflationary force." Regarding inflation metrics, she highlighted the "most important page": Truflation's real-time inflation indicator shows inflation "breaking down," with her reading at approximately 0.7% year-over-year. She further addressed marginal shifts in housing and energy: " Existing home price inflation has fallen below 1%," new home price inflation remains negative, and rents are beginning to decline; oil prices show a "double-digit year-over-year drop," which she described as equivalent to a "tax cut" for consumers and businesses. Labor Market Pain and Entrepreneurial Wave Addressing the current low consumer confidence, Wood acknowledged that consumers "are not happy," primarily due to genuine weakness in the labor market and a crisis in housing affordability. "Last year's employment figures were revised downward by 861,000, which roughly equates to a monthly loss of 75,000 jobs," Wood noted, explaining why consumer sentiment diverged from GDP data. However, she found a silver lining in youth unemployment figures. While the unemployment rate for those aged 16-24 had surged, it had recently fallen below 10%. Wood believes this represents not merely job recovery, but likely an "Entrepreneurial Explosion" fueled by AI. "AI has become so powerful that individuals can now directly go out and build businesses." She predicts that as AI tools become widespread, a surge of highly efficient startups driven by individuals or small teams will emerge, serving as another crucial engine for productivity growth.
